frameworks.name: drift-diffusion model
frameworks.one_line: evidence-accumulation model parameterising perceptual decisions, here used to localise the tRNS effect

frameworks.name: stochastic resonance
frameworks.one_line: added noise enhances subthreshold signal quality in nonlinear sensory systems

frameworks.name: transcranial random noise stimulation
frameworks.one_line: noise injected into visual cortex as the experimental instantiation of stochastic resonance

core_question: Does adding random noise to visual cortex via tRNS obey stochastic-resonance principles during perceptual decision-making, and which decision component does it modulate?
key_assumptions: perceptual decisions arise from gradual accumulation of noisy sensory evidence
an optimal level of added noise can enhance subthreshold signals in nonlinear neural systems
drift-diffusion parameters dissociate sensory evidence quality from non-decision components
manipulations: presence vs absence of bilateral visual-cortex tRNS
motion-coherence level relative to perceptual threshold (well-below, just-below, above)
key_findings: bilateral tRNS improved motion-direction judgements only for just-subthreshold stimuli
no behavioural benefit when stimuli were well below or above threshold
drift-diffusion fits attributed the benefit specifically to an increased drift rate
interpretations: human perceptual decisions follow stochastic-resonance principles when cortical noise is added externally
tRNS acts on the rate of sensory evidence accumulation rather than other decision components
